title: Sample preparation and imaging procedures for fast and multiplexed superresolution microscopy with DNA-PAINT-ERS
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"rt1ut","text":"DNA point accumulation for imaging in nanoscale topography (DNA-PAINT) facilitates multiplexing in superresolution microscopy but is practically limited by slow imaging speed. We have developed DNA-PAINT-ERS, where E=ethylene carbonate, R=repeating sequence, and S=spacer, for fast and multiplexed superresolution imaging with DNA-PAINT. Here we describe detailed procedures for DNA-PAINT-ERS including reagent preparation, sample labeling, as well as image acqusition and analysis.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qxls7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"fxo7q","text":"DNA-PAINT is a recent technique for superresolution imaging based on subdiffractive localization of single fluorescent events1. In DNA-PAINT, the single-molecule events are due to transient binding and unbinding between a pair of DNA oligos, one attached to the affinity agent bound to a specific target and designated the docking strand (DS), and the other conjugated to a fluorophore and designated the imaging strand (IS). The fluorescent IS typically diffuses in the imaging buffer and is not visible until it hybridizes with the DS, upon which a distinct, fluorescent spot is detected on the camera. As such, each fluorescent spot arises from a single IS molecule binding to the DS, and the precise location of the DS (and the underlying target molecule) can be determined through subpixel localization of the single-molecule image. Compared with other superresolution microscopy strategies2,3, DNA-PAINT has the advantage of convenient multiplexing: multiple targets can each be labeled with a DNA-barcoded affinity agent (e.g. antibody) and imaged sequentially using corresponding IS oligos in multiple cycles. Since the binding between each pair of IS and DS is transient, the IS used for the current target can be easily and cleanly removed via buffer exchange, and the sample is ready for the next round of imaging using an IS for another target. In practice, however, the use of DNA-PAINT had been hampered by the slow imaging speed, with each imaging session typically taking tens of minutes to hours.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":125,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":895,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vpuly","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"sa8cd","text":"To overcome this limitation, we have introduced a set of strategies for accelerating multiplexed DNA-PAINT imaging. These strategies comprise the addition of ethylene carbonate, a small molecule that increases DS-IS dissociation (off-) rate with little impact on the association (on-) rate, the usage of repetitive docking sequences in the DS to increase IS binding, and the insertion of a short spacer between the DS and the affinity agent to reduce steric hindrance and increase IS binding4. These strategies, collectively termed DNA-PAINT-ERS (E=ethylene carbonate, R=repeating sequence, S=spacer), allows completion of superresolution imaging in 2-5 minutes. DNA-PAINT-ERS uses existing DS-IS pairs and workflows with minor modifications that can be easily implemented. Below we provide detailed, step-by-step instructions for performing single- or dual-color DNA-PAINT-ERS imaging of cellular structures including microtubules, clathrin, and caveolae. The procedures can be readily adapted for imaging other targets and in more colors.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":491,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"mdlpv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

UV-Vis spectrometer such as Nanodrop 2000 (ThermoFisher) for meausring concentrations of DNA, protein, and fluorophores;","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dxu7f","text":"4. Tissue culture incubator and biosafety hood;","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3sua5","text":"5. Commercial or custom single-molecule superresolution microscopy setup. As an example, the custom superresolution microsocope setup used in the demonstrations in this protcol comprises the following: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"enmfv","text":"Three lasers emitting at 488 nm (Coherent Sapphire 488, 200 mW), 561 nm (Opto Engine LLC, 150 mW), and 647 nm (Coherent OBIS 647, 140 mW) were combined and introduced into the back of a Nikon Ti-U microscope equipped with a 60× TIRF objective (Nikon, Oil immersion, NA 1.49). An f=400 mm lens was placed at the back port of the microscope to focus the collimated laser light to the back aperture of the objective to achieve through-objective total internal reflection (TIR) illumination. The excitation light can be continuously tuned between epi-fluorescence and strict TIR modes by shifting the incident laser horizontally with a translational stage before entering the back port of the microscope. A custom focus stabilizing system based on detection of the reflected excitation laser was used to stabilize the focus during data acquisition. A multi-edge polychroic mirror (Semrock, Di01-R405/488/561/635) was used to reflect the lasers into the objective and clean up fluorescence signals from the sample. Emission filters used for the 488 nm (for imaging FAM on the DS), 561 nm (for imaging Cy3 on the DS), and 647 nm (for imaging CF660R conjugated imager strands) were FF01-525/45, FF01-605/64, FF01-708/75, respectively (all from Semrock). Fluorescence signals were collected through the objective by an electron-multiplied charge-coupled device (EM-CCD, Andor, iXon Ultra 897) using a typical EM gain setting at 200-300 in frame transfer mode.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jef7m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cvfqg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Suspend DS oligos in ultra-pure water for a 1 mM concentration

Combine 20 µl of DS oligo (1 mM), 19 µl of ultra-pure water, and 8.5 µl of freshly made 1M NaHCO 3 . Vortex, then add 2.5 µl of desired DBCO-PEGx-NHS ester linker (DBCO-PEG12-NHS ester, DBCO-PEG4-NHS ester, or DBCO-Sulfo-NHS; 10 mM stocks in DMSO). Vortex and run reaction for 3 hours at room temperature with gentle shaking
Add 500 µl of 100% EtOH and 50 µl of 3M sodium acetate. Vortex, then leave in -80°C overnight
Spin for 30 min at ~20,000 g at 4°C
Remove supernatant, add 500 µl ETOH, vortex, and spin for 5 min at ~20,000 g in 4°C. Repeat 3 times
After the last wash, remove supernatant and add 50 µl of ultra-pure water, 100% EtOH and 50 µl of 3M sodium acetate. Vortex, then leave in -80°C overnight
Repeat DNA centrifugation and purification once as described earlier
Measure DS-PEG concentration with NanoDrop UV-Vis spectrophotometer and record ratio of absorbance values at 260 nm (for DNA) and 495 nm (for 6-FAM) or 550 nm (for Cy3™)
Make a 10 mg/ml stock of CF®660R Succinimidyl Ester (or another NHS-ester of another dye of choice) in DMSO; store as aliquots and keep at -80°C
Add 5 µl of IS oligo (1 mM) to 40.5 µl of freshly made 0.1 M NaHCO 3 . Vortex, then add 4.5 µl of dye (10 mg/ml). Vortex and run reaction for 3 hours at room temperature with gentle shaking
Add 500 µl of 100% EtOH and 50 µl of 3M sodium acetate. Vortex, then leave in -80°C overnight
Spin for 30 min at ~20,000 g at 4°C
Remove supernatant, add 500 µl ETOH, vortex, and spin for 5 min, ~20,000 g, 4°C. Repeat 3 times
After the last wash, remove supernatant and dry pellet. Add 20 µl of ultra-pure water to the final product
Measure IS concentration with NanoDrop UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Calculate DOL using 260 nm and 660 nm absorbance values
Mix 100 µl of antibody with 10 µl of freshly made 1 M NaHCO 3
Add azido-PEG 4 -NHS in 100x molar excess (around 1 µl if using 100 mM stocks in DMSO)
Vortex and run reaction for 3 hours at room temperature with gentle shaking
Flow antibody-PEG 4 -azide conjugates through a 50 kDa size exclusion column and wash with PBS 15 times on the column via centrifugation. Centrifuge at 4°C, 6000 g, 2.5 minutes each spin
After the final wash, there should be at least 90 µl of antibody-azide remaining
Add DS-PEGx-DBCO in 5x molar excess to the antibody-azide conjugate. Vortex and run reaction overnight at room temperature with gentle shaking
Add 0.1% sodium azide to quench reaction (at ~1:10 v/v ratio to the reaction mixture)

After one hour, flow antibody-DS conjugates through a 100 kDa size exclusion column and wash with PBS 5 times on the column via centrifugation. Centrifuge at 4°C, 6000 g, 2.5 minutes each spin. The final product (antibody-PEG 4+x -DS) should be suspended in ~90 µl PBS

Measure antibody concentration with NanoDrop UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Calculate DOL using peak absorbance values at 280 nm (for DNA), 495 nm (for 6-FAM) or 550 nm (for Cy3™), correcting for DNA absorbance contribution at 260 nm
Maintain U2OS cells in Gibco DMEM + 10% FBS and passage every three to four days
Grow cells overnight on 8-well chambered coverglass in phenol red-free DMEM for 50-60% confluency the next day

For immunostaining of clathrin or caveolin, quickly wash cells with PBS 1x and then fix with cold 3.7% paraformaldehyde (PFA) in 1x PHEM buffer. Recipe for 2x PHEM buffer: 0.06M PIPES, 0.025M HEPES, 0.01M EGTA, 0.008M MgSO4 in distilled water, with pH adjusted to 7 with 10M potassium hydroxide
After two PBS washes, quench with freshly made 0.1% sodium borohydride in PBS for 7 minutes
Wash with PBS 3 times and permeabilize with 0.5% saponin in PBS for 20 minutes

For immunostaining of microtubules, fix cells with 3.7% PFA and 0.1% glutaraldehyde (GA) in 1x PHEM for 20 min before quenching with sodium borohydride and permeabilization in 0.2% Triton X-100 in PBS.
Block in 5% BSA in PBS for 30 minutes on a gentle rocker

Incubate with the primary antibodies for clathrin, caveolin, or tubulin antibody (0.5 mg/mL or 1:200 dilution from stock) in PBS buffer containing 3% BSA and 5% salmon sperm DNA. Let incubation take place on a rocker at room temperature for 1 hour

Wash with PBS (3x, 5 minutes each) before incubation with DS-conjugated secondary antibody at a final concentration of ~8 µg/ml in PBS buffer containing 3% BSA and 5% salmon sperm DNA. Let incubation take place on a rocker at room temperature for 1 hour. Keep sample in the dark from here on out
Wash with PBS (3x, 5 minutes each)

For immunostaining in extracted samples (microtubules alone, or microtubules co-labeled with clathrin), pre-permeabilize cells with cold 0.1% Triton X-100 in 1x PHEM buffer for 45 seconds. Then fix with 3.7% PFA in 1x PHEM for 20 minutes
After two PBS washes, quench with freshly made 0.1% sodium borohydride in PBS for 7 minutes
For microtubule single staining, follow procedure described earlier for unextracted samples
For co-stained samples, further permeabilize the cells and block with 3% BSA and 0.2% Triton X-100 for 1 hour
Label cells with beta-tubulin as described earlier, postfix with 3.7% PFA for 10 minutes, and then stain for clathrin as described earlier.
For all cell staining procedures, post-fix for 10 minutes with 3.7% PFA and 0.1% GA in 1x PHEM
Before imaging, add 150 µl of 2.5% 50 nm gold particles in PBS+ to cells for 10 minutes. Wash with PBS

Add imaging buffer with desired ratios of Buffer C (500 mM), ethylene carbonate, and IS-CF660R at 1-2 nM final concentration. The exact concentration of IS may need to be adjusted depending on the target and based on the imaging kinetics.

Image acquisition should be done accordingly to the configurations of the microscope system used. Here we describe procedures for operating a custom single-molecule superresolution setup based on a Nikon Ti-U microscope frame. Specifics of a custom superresolution setup can be found in the 'Equipment' section

Image analysis and reconstruction for DNA-PAINT could also be performed with custom software including those in the public domain 5 . Here we describe . More details can be found in our recent publication by Nickerson et al. 6
Turn on the microscope, lasers and EMCCD camera. Select 60x Apochromat TIRF objective with 1.49NA and mount the sample by applying index matching oil between the objective and sample
Open the laser shutter and bring the sample to focus
Adjust TIRF angle such that desired penetration depth is reached

Initially set the exposure time (30-50 ms), laser power (~500 W/cm 2 ), EMCCD gain (200-300), and adjust these parameters as necessary depending on the single-molecule brightness and localization kinetics
Engage the auto-focus system and start the acquisition using micromanager 7 or another acquisition software of choice
Extract the localizations, sorting, and rendering by using in-house Matlab scripts 6
creator: Protocols Importer
